title: How to Win Friends and Influence People
author: Dale Carnegie

parts.label: Part Two
parts.title: Six Ways to Make People Like You

parts.label: Part Three
parts.title: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking

parts.label: Part Four
parts.title: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment

parts.label: Part Six
parts.title: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier

chapters.partTitle: Fundamental Techniques in Handling People
chapters.title: If You Want to Gather Honey, Don’t Kick Over the Beehive
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that criticism almost never produces honest change because people defend their pride before they examine their behavior. If you want cooperation, start by understanding why the person acted as they did and keep blame out of your opening move.
chapters.keyStory: Two-Gun Crowley, Al Capone, and Abraham Lincoln's unsent letter to General Meade.
chapters.principle: Don't criticize, condemn, or complain.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Criticism is dangerous because it wounds pride and invites self-justification.
chapters.misconception: People misread this chapter as a ban on standards. Carnegie is not saying to ignore bad behavior; he is saying that blame is a poor way to correct it.

chapters.partTitle: Fundamental Techniques in Handling People
chapters.title: The Big Secret of Dealing with People
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says the practical lever in human relations is not pressure but the other person's desire to feel important. The actionable move is to notice and name something genuinely admirable, because honest appreciation motivates effort while flattery creates suspicion.
chapters.keyStory: Charles Schwab using praise to lift output; Andrew Carnegie elevating people through recognition.
chapters.principle: Give honest and sincere appreciation.
chapters.quoteCandidate: The deepest urge in human nature is the desire to be important.
chapters.misconception: Readers often flatten this into generic positivity. Carnegie is drawing a hard line between earned appreciation and manipulative flattery.

chapters.partTitle: Fundamental Techniques in Handling People
chapters.title: He Who Can Do This Has the Whole World with Him - He Who Cannot Walks a Lonely Way
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's claim is blunt: people move for their reasons, not yours. To influence action, describe the outcome in terms of the other person's wants, pride, convenience, or ambition, and let your ask ride on that motive instead of on duty alone.
chapters.keyStory: Parents getting a child excited for kindergarten; Carnegie's hotel ballroom negotiation.
chapters.principle: Arouse in the other person an eager want.
chapters.quoteCandidate: First, arouse in the other person an eager want.
chapters.misconception: This is often treated as trickery. Carnegie is not advocating deception; he is insisting that persuasion begins by understanding the other person's incentives.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: Do This and You’ll Be Welcome Anywhere
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's advice here is not to be charming in the abstract but to be actively interested. Ask about the other person's world, remember details, and make your curiosity visible; people warm to you when the interaction clearly is not a performance about you.
chapters.keyStory: Howard Thurston and Theodore Roosevelt winning people by studying them first.
chapters.principle: Become genuinely interested in other people.
chapters.quoteCandidate: You can make more friends in two months by becoming interested in other people than in two years by trying to get them interested in you.
chapters.misconception: The chapter is not telling you to collect networking tricks. Carnegie is arguing for disciplined attention, not social performance.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: A Simple Way to Make a Good First Impression
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that a smile changes the emotional temperature before any words matter. The actionable point is to make warmth visible on your face so people do not have to guess whether you are irritated, guarded, or pleased to meet them.
chapters.keyStory: Charles Schwab's million-dollar smile; William Steinhardt's week-long smile experiment.
chapters.principle: Smile.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Actions seem to follow feeling, but feeling often follows action.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes hear this as fake cheerfulness. Carnegie's real point is that visible warmth changes the opening conditions of the interaction.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: If You Don’t Do This, You Are Headed for Trouble
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie treats a person's name as a shortcut to dignity and attention. The practical rule is to hear it carefully, remember it, and use it naturally, because forgetting or mangling it tells the other person they are generic to you.
chapters.keyStory: Jim Farley remembering thousands of names; Andrew Carnegie naming things after people.
chapters.principle: Remember that a person's name is, to that person, the sweetest and most important sound in any language.
chapters.quoteCandidate: A person's name is the sweetest and most important sound in any language.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is not about memory stunts for show. Carnegie is connecting names to respect and personal recognition.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: An Easy Way to Become a Good Conversationalist
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says most people do not want a dazzling talker; they want a listener who helps them say what matters to them. Ask questions that let the other person expand, and resist the urge to show how interesting you are.
chapters.keyStory: The bridge-party guest who listened while another guest talked about Africa; the angry customer who softened once heard.
chapters.principle: Be a good listener. Encourage others to talk about themselves.
chapters.quoteCandidate: If you want to know how to make people shun you, talk only about yourself.
chapters.misconception: People often confuse listening with passive silence. Carnegie means active listening that invites detail and relief.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: How to Interest People
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's point is tactical: before you ask for anything, find the subject the other person already cares about and enter there. If you research their hobby, role, or obsession first, your conversation stops feeling like an extraction attempt and starts feeling relevant.
chapters.keyStory: Theodore Roosevelt reading up on every visitor's interests; William Lyon Phelps on being fascinated by another person's passion.
chapters.principle: Talk in terms of the other person's interests.
chapters.quoteCandidate: The royal road to a person's heart is to talk about the things he or she treasures most.
chapters.misconception: This is not small talk optimization. Carnegie is telling you to study what the other person values before you make your move.

chapters.partTitle: Six Ways to Make People Like You
chapters.title: How to Make People Like You Instantly
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie is not recommending empty compliments; he is saying people respond to sincere signals that you see value in them. The move is to voice a specific, honest appreciation that respects the other person's pride instead of trying to impress them with your own status.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie complimenting the postal clerk's hair and immediately changing the interaction.
chapters.principle: Make the other person feel important, and do it sincerely.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Talk to people about themselves and they will listen for hours.
chapters.misconception: Readers often reduce this to praise. Carnegie is actually targeting the deeper need to feel respected and significant.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: You Can’t Win an Argument
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's argument is that even when you win on facts, you often lose on pride, trust, and future cooperation. The practical discipline is to step out of argument mode early, especially when the other person is emotionally invested in not losing face.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie correcting a banquet guest over a quotation while Sir Ross Smith watched the exchange.
chapters.principle: The only way to get the best of an argument is to avoid it.
chapters.quoteCandidate: A person convinced against his will is of the same opinion still.
chapters.misconception: This is not an anti-truth chapter. Carnegie is distinguishing between proving a point and getting buy-in.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: A Sure Way of Making Enemies and How to Avoid It
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says 'you're wrong' is rarely an act of correction; it is usually an attack on competence and judgment. A better move is to say you may be mistaken, invite a look at the facts, and let the other person change position without public humiliation.
chapters.keyStory: Benjamin Franklin softening his language; advisers who lost influence by blunt contradiction.
chapters.principle: Show respect for the other person's opinions. Never say, 'You're wrong.'
chapters.quoteCandidate: I may be wrong. Let's examine the facts.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes hear this as weakness. Carnegie is after influence, not self-erasure.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: If You’re Wrong, Admit It
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie advises admitting fault before the other person has to force it out of you. Quick, emphatic ownership drains the fight out of the room, because it satisfies the other person's need for acknowledgment and makes excuses unnecessary.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie conceding fault to a dog-licensing officer instead of escalating the confrontation.
chapters.principle: If you are wrong, admit it quickly and emphatically.
chapters.quoteCandidate: By fighting you never get enough, but by yielding you get more than you expected.
chapters.misconception: This is not about ritual apology. Carnegie wants early ownership because it changes the emotional shape of the conflict.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: The High Road to a Man’s Reason
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's claim is that reason does not enter a conversation through force; it enters through goodwill. If you want the other person to hear your case, begin in a friendly way that lowers defensiveness before you ask them to reconsider anything.
chapters.keyStory: John D. Rockefeller Jr. addressing striking miners with respect and calm.
chapters.principle: Begin in a friendly way.
chapters.quoteCandidate: A drop of honey catches more flies than a gallon of gall.
chapters.misconception: Readers often mistake friendliness for softness. Carnegie means strategic warmth that keeps the other person available to reason.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: The Secret of Socrates
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's actionable lesson is to start where agreement already exists and stack small yeses before you ask for a larger one. Once someone has moved into a rejecting 'no' posture, their pride starts defending that posture even against facts.
chapters.keyStory: Socrates' question method and Professor Overstreet's explanation of why early 'no' answers harden resistance.
chapters.principle: Get the other person saying 'yes, yes' immediately.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Start a person in the affirmative direction.
chapters.misconception: This is not a trick sequence of canned yeses. Carnegie is showing how momentum and self-consistency shape persuasion.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: The Safety Valve in Handling Complaints
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that complaints often have to be expressed before they can be resolved. Ask questions, let the person empty the grievance fully, and do not rush to counterattack while they are still trying to be heard.
chapters.keyStory: The fabric salesman who mostly listened while a dissatisfied buyer talked himself toward agreement.
chapters.principle: Let the other person do a great deal of the talking.
chapters.quoteCandidate: If you want enemies, outshine your friends; if you want friends, let them outshine you.
chapters.misconception: People flatten this into passive listening. Carnegie is describing a deliberate release valve that makes cooperation possible.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: How to Get Co-Operation
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says people resist being sold or ordered, but they support conclusions they feel they reached themselves. The practical move is to structure the discussion so the other person states the standards, terms, or solution out loud and then feels responsible for them.
chapters.keyStory: Adolph Seltz asking his sales team to define mutual expectations instead of imposing them.
chapters.principle: Let the other person feel that the idea is his or hers.
chapters.quoteCandidate: People have more faith in ideas they discover for themselves.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is not about stealing credit. Carnegie wants genuine participation because ownership drives commitment.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: A Formula That Will Work Wonders for You
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's formula is disciplined empathy: stop asking whether the other person is wrong and ask why their reaction makes sense from where they stand. If you can name the pressure, fear, or incentive shaping their view, you stop arguing with the surface and start addressing the cause.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie's repeated use of perspective-taking in negotiations and sales settings.
chapters.principle: Try honestly to see things from the other person's point of view.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Success in dealing with people depends on a sympathetic grasp of the other person's viewpoint.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes reduce this to kindness. Carnegie is making a diagnostic argument: perspective-taking improves accuracy.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: What Everybody Wants
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie recommends saying the sentence most people rarely hear in conflict: 'If I were you, I'd feel the same way.' That move does not surrender your position; it shows the other person you understand their emotional logic well enough to stop fighting their feelings first.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie's 'magic phrase' for irritated customers and resistant counterparts.
chapters.principle: Be sympathetic with the other person's ideas and desires.
chapters.quoteCandidate: If I were you, I should undoubtedly feel just as you do.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is not permission to indulge every demand. Carnegie is teaching emotional acknowledgment as a precondition for influence.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: An Appeal That Everybody Likes
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says people usually want to see themselves as fair, generous, and principled, even when mixed motives are present. If you frame your request around the decent motive they can respect, you give them a socially and morally attractive reason to cooperate.
chapters.keyStory: J. P. Morgan's line about everyone having two reasons; Carnegie's stories about appealing to fair play and honor.
chapters.principle: Appeal to the nobler motives.
chapters.quoteCandidate: People are idealists at heart.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes hear this as sentimental. Carnegie is describing how self-image can be used to pull action upward.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: The Movies Do It. Radio Does It. Why Don’t You Do It?
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's point is that truth stated flatly often loses to distraction, skepticism, and inertia. To move people, make the idea concrete and dramatic enough that they can see the stakes, not just hear a claim.
chapters.keyStory: Sales and publicity examples where dramatized demonstration beat plain explanation.
chapters.principle: Dramatize your ideas.
chapters.quoteCandidate: The truth has to be made vivid, interesting, dramatic.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is not a license for theatrics without substance. Carnegie is saying good ideas often need better delivery.

chapters.partTitle: Twelve Ways to Win People to Your Way of Thinking
chapters.title: When Nothing Else Works, Try This
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says pride, competition, and the desire to excel can motivate where explanation and pressure fail. When people have skill or spirit, turning the work into a challenge can wake up effort that orders and warnings never reached.
chapters.keyStory: Charles Schwab chalking production numbers on the mill floor to spark rivalry between shifts.
chapters.principle: Throw down a challenge.
chapters.quoteCandidate: The way to get things done is to stimulate competition.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes turn this into macho pressure. Carnegie is pointing to challenge as energizing work, not humiliating people.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: If You Must Find Fault, This Is the Way to Begin
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's advice is not to sugarcoat criticism mechanically, but to make sure the person first feels seen for what is working. Genuine praise lowers the pain of correction and keeps the feedback from sounding like a total verdict on the person.
chapters.keyStory: Calvin Coolidge and other leaders who opened correction with praise instead of attack.
chapters.principle: Begin with praise and honest appreciation.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Beginning with praise is like the dentist starting with Novocain.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is often used to justify manipulative compliment sandwiches. Carnegie's real concern is preserving receptivity, not scripting fake niceness.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: How to Criticize and Not Be Hated for It
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that direct accusation often creates denial where a tactful cue would create adjustment. The move is to make the person notice the mistake without forcing them into public self-defense.
chapters.keyStory: Charles Schwab handing cigars to workers under a no-smoking sign; John Wanamaker quietly correcting staff behavior.
chapters.principle: Call attention to people's mistakes indirectly.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Many people begin criticism with sincere praise followed by the word 'but.'
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes think indirectness means vagueness. Carnegie means tactful clarity, not leaving the issue unclear.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: Talk About Your Own Mistakes First
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie wants the critic to give up moral altitude before offering correction. When you admit your own earlier blind spots or immature mistakes, the other person hears guidance instead of superiority.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie correcting Josephine Carnegie by first naming his own youthful mistakes; Prince von Bulow repairing damage with Kaiser Wilhelm II.
chapters.principle: Talk about your own mistakes before criticizing the other person.
chapters.quoteCandidate: It is easier to listen to unpleasant things after we have heard some humility.
chapters.misconception: This is not confessional theater. Carnegie uses self-disclosure to reduce defensiveness and restore teachability.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: No One Likes to Take Orders
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's rule here is about preserving agency. Ask questions, invite options, and let the other person participate in the fix so the change feels collaborative rather than imposed.
chapters.keyStory: Owen D. Young leading through suggestions and questions instead of direct orders.
chapters.principle: Ask questions instead of giving direct orders.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Questions make an order more palatable and often stimulate creativity.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes hear this as indecisive leadership. Carnegie is arguing that agency often produces better execution than command.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: Let the Other Man Save His Face
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that embarrassment is often a larger wound than the decision itself. Even when someone must be corrected, demoted, or dismissed, preserve their dignity so the outcome does not become a needless humiliation.
chapters.keyStory: General Electric finding a dignified new role for Charles Steinmetz; tactful layoffs and reassignments.
chapters.principle: Let the other person save face.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Even if we are right and the other person is definitely wrong, we only destroy ego by causing humiliation.
chapters.misconception: This is not avoidance. Carnegie is saying consequences can be delivered without stripping someone of dignity.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: How to Spur Men on to Success
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says improvement sticks better when people are rewarded for progress than when they are punished for imperfection. Praise even small gains specifically, because visible progress changes effort from reluctant to self-reinforcing.
chapters.keyStory: Animal trainers rewarding desired behavior; supervisors who built confidence by praising progress.
chapters.principle: Praise the slightest improvement and praise every improvement. Be hearty in your approbation and lavish in your praise.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Abilities wither under criticism and bloom under encouragement.
chapters.misconception: Readers often dismiss this as soft feedback. Carnegie is pointing to reinforcement as a serious performance tool.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: Give a Dog a Good Name
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's point is that people often become the role they believe you see in them. If you tell someone, credibly, that they are dependable, meticulous, or capable, they frequently work to stay consistent with that description.
chapters.keyStory: Mrs. Ernest Gent turning Nellie into a better housekeeper by treating her as naturally neat; Henry Henke praising Bill's skill.
chapters.principle: Give the other person a fine reputation to live up to.
chapters.quoteCandidate: If you want to improve a person, act as though that particular trait were already one of their outstanding characteristics.
chapters.misconception: This chapter is not about empty labels. Carnegie is describing identity-based encouragement that comes with believable expectations.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: Make the Fault Seem Easy to Correct
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says discouragement often comes less from the fault than from the belief that the fault defines the person. Encourage as if the improvement is well within reach, and people will attempt changes they would reject if framed as proof of inadequacy.
chapters.keyStory: Teachers, coaches, and managers framing weaknesses as correctable rather than fixed.
chapters.principle: Use encouragement. Make the fault seem easy to correct.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Let the person know that you have faith in the ability to improve.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes confuse this with lowering standards. Carnegie means preserve standards while protecting confidence.

chapters.partTitle: Nine Ways to Change People Without Giving Offense or Arousing Resentment
chapters.title: Making People Glad to Do What You Want
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's final leadership move is to connect your request to a role, benefit, or pride point the other person can feel good about accepting. If they can leave the conversation feeling important, useful, or trusted, cooperation stops feeling like surrender.
chapters.keyStory: Colonel House handling William Jennings Bryan; adults getting children to help by giving them meaningful roles.
chapters.principle: Make the other person happy about doing the thing you suggest.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Always make the other person happy about doing the thing you suggest.
chapters.misconception: This is not bribery disguised as leadership. Carnegie is showing how to align requests with identity and pride.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: How to Dig Your Marital Grave in the Quickest Possible Way
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that constant fault-finding at home destroys admiration by a thousand cuts. The actionable rule is to stop nagging and stop turning daily irritation into a running commentary on your spouse's defects.
chapters.keyStory: Mary Todd Lincoln's relentless criticism of Abraham Lincoln and the damage chronic fault-finding can do.
chapters.principle: Don't nag.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Nagging is one of the most potent destroyers of affection.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes reduce this to 'never raise issues at home.' Carnegie is targeting repeated corrosive criticism, not necessary conversation.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: Love and Let Live
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's marriage advice here is not tolerance in the abstract but non-interference with harmless differences. If you spend your energy remodeling your partner's tastes, habits, or temperament, you convert intimacy into inspection.
chapters.keyStory: Benjamin and Mary Anne Disraeli building affection without trying to remake each other.
chapters.principle: Don't try to make your partner over.
chapters.quoteCandidate: The first thing to learn in intercourse with others is non-interference with their own peculiar ways of being happy.
chapters.misconception: This is not approval of harmful behavior. Carnegie is addressing the urge to manage personality rather than love the person in front of you.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: Do This and You’ll Be Looking Up the Time-Tables to Reno
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie says ordinary marriage breakdown often comes less from dramatic betrayal than from repeated criticism. If home becomes the place where a person is most judged, the relationship starts training both people to brace instead of relax.
chapters.keyStory: Carnegie's Reno-divorce framing and the steady damage of harsh domestic criticism.
chapters.principle: Don't criticize.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Any fool can criticize, condemn, and complain, and most fools do.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes think this repeats Chapter 1 without nuance. Carnegie is applying the no-criticism rule to the most intimate setting, where its violation hurts more.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: A Quick Way to Make Everybody Happy
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie argues that spouses often let effort disappear into assumption. The practical correction is to say appreciation out loud, specifically and often, so everyday labor does not become invisible.
chapters.keyStory: Eddie Cantor crediting his wife; Warner Baxter insisting appreciation sustains happiness.
chapters.principle: Give honest appreciation.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Give the little girl a great big hand.
chapters.misconception: This is not about grand romantic gestures. Carnegie is emphasizing spoken appreciation for ordinary contributions.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: They Mean So Much to a Woman
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie's point is that affection is maintained through small signals: remembering dates, bringing flowers, making calls, noticing details. Love often does not die in one blow; it erodes because kindness was postponed too many times.
chapters.keyStory: George M. Cohan's regular calls to his mother and Judge Joseph Sabbath on the 'trivialities' behind domestic conflict.
chapters.principle: Pay little attentions.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Love is not kept alive by one dazzling act but by many small ones.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes dismiss this as old-fashioned etiquette. Carnegie is making a compounding argument about repeated care.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: If You Want to Be Happy, Don’t Neglect This One
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie warns that many couples become ruder to each other than they would ever be to strangers. The actionable rule is simple but demanding: keep ordinary politeness alive inside the home, especially when familiarity tempts you to be careless.
chapters.keyStory: Walter Damrosch and his wife modeling lasting courtesy in marriage.
chapters.principle: Be courteous.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Courtesy is as important in marriage as grease is to machinery.
chapters.misconception: People hear courtesy as superficial. Carnegie means disciplined respect in the places where people are most likely to get lazy.

chapters.partTitle: Seven Rules for Making Your Home Life Happier
chapters.title: Don’t Be a “Marriage Illiterate”
chapters.coreConcept: Carnegie closes by saying marriage should not rely on ignorance about sex and physical compatibility. Couples need to treat intimate knowledge as something to learn responsibly, because goodwill alone does not solve preventable confusion and dissatisfaction.
chapters.keyStory: Dr. Katherine Bement Davis and Dr. G. V. Hamilton on widespread marital unhappiness rooted in ignorance.
chapters.principle: Don't be a 'marriage illiterate'; learn the sexual side of marriage responsibly.
chapters.quoteCandidate: Goodwill is not enough when ignorance governs intimacy.
chapters.misconception: Readers sometimes treat this chapter as a historical curiosity. Carnegie intended it as a practical claim that relationship skill includes informed intimacy.
